aircraft.identity: The largest passenger aircraft ever built.
prose: The A380 is the only full-length double-deck airliner ever to enter service — not a hump like the 747, but two complete passenger decks from nose to tail. That single decision drives everything else about it: the 24-metre tail, the 80-metre wingspan that forced airports to rebuild gates, and a maximum weight of 575 tonnes, more than any passenger aircraft before or since.

Commercially it was a bet that air travel would concentrate into mega-hubs. It mostly didn't — the market moved to smaller twins flying point to point — and production ended in 2021 after 251 aircraft. But the fleet that exists is loved: passengers pick flights to be on it, and Emirates built its entire long-haul model around it.
In this catalog the A380's double deck shows up directly in the numbers: it carries more seats per metre of fuselage than anything else flying.

aircraft.identity: The world's largest twinjet — the long-haul workhorse.
prose: The 777 is the aircraft that quietly retired the jumbo jet. Boeing designed it in the early 1990s as the first airliner developed entirely on computers, and gave it two engines so large — the GE90's fan is wider than a 737's fuselage — that a twin could finally do what only three- and four-engine aircraft had done before.

The -300ER variant became the best-selling widebody of its era because the economics were brutal in its favour: near-747 capacity on two engines instead of four. Around 1,750 777s have been built across all variants, and the freighter version carries a huge share of the world's long-haul air cargo.
The 777X, with folding wingtips and the even larger GE9X, is the family's next chapter — in certification now.

prose: No airliner has been built in greater numbers than the 737 — roughly 11,700 across four generations since 1967, with thousands more on order. If you have flown, you have almost certainly flown on one. It is short, low to the ground (a 1960s decision to suit airports with no jet bridges), and unmistakable in profile: the stubby dropped nose and the dorsal fin fillet along the spine.

That low stance became the family's defining engineering constraint. Every new generation of larger engines had to be squeezed under the same short landing gear — which is why 737 nacelles are famously flattened at the bottom, and why the MAX's bigger LEAP engines had to be mounted further forward and higher.
The numbers here are for the MAX 8, the current generation's core variant. The family spans everything from the 85-seat -500 to the 230-seat MAX 10.

operatorsOther.note: More than 475 further airlines worldwide fly the 737 — no airliner has a broader operator base. Counts on this page are fleet-report approximations pending the full verification pass the A380 and A350 lists received.

prose: The A350 is what an airliner looks like when you start again with carbon fibre: over half its structure is composite, the wing flexes visibly in flight and curves up into a raked sharklet, and the whole aircraft is built around burning dramatically less fuel per seat than the generation it replaced.

The -1000 is the stretch, aimed squarely at the 777-300ER, and the -900ULR flies the longest scheduled routes in the world — Singapore to New York, nearly nineteen hours nonstop. Qatar Airways launched the type in 2015 and remains one of its defining operators.
The spec shown here is the A350-1000; the freighter A350F is in development.
aircraft.operatorsOnOrder: Coming to the type: United Airlines (45), IndiGo (60+), KLM (50), Riyadh Air (25), EVA Air (18), Air Europa (40), Air Canada (8), plus A350F freighter customers including Atlas Air, CMA CGM, Martinair and Silk Way West.

aircraft.name: Antonov An-225 Mriya
aircraft.headline: The largest aircraft ever built — and the longest, until war destroyed the only one.
prose: Mriya means "dream", and the An-225 was built for exactly one: to carry the Soviet Buran space shuttle on its back. Antonov took the already-enormous An-124, stretched it, added two more engines for a total of six, doubled the landing gear to 32 wheels, and replaced the single fin with a twin tail so the shuttle's wake wouldn't hammer it. The result was the heaviest aircraft ever flown — 640 tonnes at maximum weight — and at 84 metres, the longest.

Buran flew only once before the programme was shelved in the early 1990s, and the An-225 went on to a second career as the world's ultimate outsize freighter. When something was too big or too heavy for anything else — 190-tonne generators, wind turbine blades, relief cargo by the hundreds of tonnes — Mriya was the phone call. It held over 200 payload-related world records, and everywhere it landed, crowds came to watch.

The sole completed An-225 was destroyed at Hostomel Airport outside Kyiv in February 2022, caught in its hangar mid-maintenance during the fighting there. A second airframe, roughly one-third complete since the 1990s, still exists, and Antonov has said Mriya may one day be rebuilt. Its records remain unbeaten.

aircraft.name: Boeing 747
aircraft.headline: The Queen of the Skies — the aircraft that invented long-haul travel for everyone.
prose: The 747 was a gamble that nearly broke Boeing. Pan Am's Juan Trippe wanted an aircraft two and a half times the size of the 707, Boeing bet the company on it, and built the world's largest building in Everett to assemble it. The famous hump exists because the 747 was designed with a hinged nose for a freighter future — the cockpit had to go somewhere, so it went upstairs, and the space behind it became the most glamorous cabin in aviation.

For 37 years it was the largest passenger aircraft in the world, and it did something bigger than any record: it made intercontinental flight affordable. Seat costs fell so far that the jet age finally opened to ordinary travellers. Every US president since 1990 has flown in one, and the freighter — the reason for the hump — still carries a huge share of the world's air cargo.

Production ended in January 2023 after 1,574 aircraft across five generations. Passenger 747s are now down to a devoted few operators, led by Lufthansa; the freighters will fly for decades. The final variant, the 747-8, remains the longest airliner in service until the 777-9 arrives — 76.25 metres of it.

aircraft.name: Airbus A340
aircraft.headline: Four engines for the long haul — and, for eight years, the longest airliner in the world.
prose: The A340 was designed for a world where twins weren't trusted over oceans. Four engines meant freedom from ETOPS restrictions — it could take the shortest route across any water, any desert, anywhere — and in the 1990s that made it the queen of the very long haul. Its marketing line was blunt: four engines for long haul.

The A340-600 of 2002 stretched the family to 75.36 metres, snatching the "world's longest airliner" title from the 747 and holding it until the 747-8 flew in 2010. But the same engines that gave it freedom became its downfall: as ETOPS rules relaxed and big twins like the 777 matched its range on half the engines, the A340's fuel economics collapsed. Production ended in 2011 after 377 aircraft — the A350 is its successor.

It flies on in smaller numbers than its Boeing rivals, led by Lufthansa's reactivated fleet, with a long tail of charter, government and VIP operators who still value what it was built for: range without restrictions.

aircraft.name: Boeing 777X
aircraft.headline: The longest airliner ever built — with folding wingtips and the biggest jet engine in history.
prose: The 777X is built around two extremes. The first is the GE9X — the largest jet engine ever made, its fan wider than an entire 737 fuselage. The second is a carbon-fibre wing so long that it wouldn't fit existing airport gates, so Boeing did something no airliner has done before: the outer 3.5 metres of each wingtip folds upward after landing, shrinking the 71.75-metre flying wingspan to 64.85 metres at the gate.

At 76.72 metres, the 777-9 will take the "longest airliner ever" title the moment it enters service — passing the 747-8 by less than half a metre. It's the 777-300ER's successor and the A350-1000's direct rival: around 426 seats in two classes, a quieter and much more efficient cabin, and the widest windows Boeing has fitted.

The programme has become famous for its delays — first flight was January 2020, and certification has slipped repeatedly, with deliveries now expected in 2027. The order book hasn't blinked: over 500 aircraft, led by Emirates' enormous commitment, with Lufthansa set to be the launch operator. A shorter ultra-long-range 777-8 and a freighter 777-8F follow.

aircraft.name: Lockheed C-5 Galaxy
aircraft.status: In service — USAF, modernised as C-5M
aircraft.headline: America's biggest aircraft — a military freighter that opens at both ends.
aircraft.identity: The largest aircraft in the US military.
prose: When the C-5 first flew in 1968 it was the largest aircraft in the world, and for decades nothing else could do its job: swallow two M1 Abrams tanks, six Apache helicopters, or any piece of Army equipment ever made, and fly it across an ocean. The nose hinges up, the tail opens wide, and vehicles drive straight through — loading at both ends simultaneously.

The airframe kneels. Its 28-wheel landing gear can lower the cargo deck to truck-bed height, one of the details that makes a 380-tonne aircraft genuinely usable in the field. The high wing droops visibly at rest — anhedral, the opposite of an airliner's upswept dihedral — and the T-tail stands nearly 20 metres up.

Fifty-two survivors have been rebuilt as the C-5M Super Galaxy, with new GE CF6 engines from the 747 world and modern avionics — good enough that the type has set dozens of records and is planned to serve into the 2040s, an eighty-year career. Its only rival for outsize cargo was the An-124, and at 75.31 metres the Galaxy remains the fifth-longest aircraft ever built.

aircraft.name: Boeing 747 Dreamlifter
aircraft.headline: The bloated 747 that exists to build another aircraft — its tail swings open sideways.
prose: The Dreamlifter exists because of a supply chain. The 787's giant composite fuselage sections are built in Japan and Italy, its wings in Nagoya — and shipping them by sea would take weeks. Boeing's answer was to take four used 747-400s and graft on a cargo hold three times the volume of a 747 freighter's, creating the bulging silhouette that makes every airport it visits stop and stare.

Its party trick is at the back: the entire tail swings open sideways on a hinge — the longest cargo loader in the world slides complete 787 fuselage barrels straight in. The conversion was engineered with Boeing by Evergreen in Taiwan, and the result is deliberately unglamorous: unpressurised in the main hold, unpainted-looking, and flown hard between Japan, Italy, South Carolina and Everett.

Only four exist, operated by Atlas Air on Boeing's behalf. During the pandemic they moonlighted hauling millions of face masks, and one earned brief fame in 2021 by landing at the wrong airport in Kansas — on a runway considered too short — and taking off again anyway. At 71.68 metres it is the ninth-longest aircraft ever built, and the fattest-looking thing in the sky.

aircraft.name: Antonov An-124 Ruslan
aircraft.status: In service — fleet split by war
aircraft.headline: The biggest production cargo aircraft in the world — and since 2022, a fleet divided by war.
prose: The An-124 is the An-225's older, smaller sibling — and unlike Mriya, it went into real production: 55 built. It out-lifts everything else still flying, with a 150-tonne payload through a nose that hinges up and a rear ramp that opens wide, plus its own onboard cranes so it can load itself at airfields with no equipment.

For three decades the world's outsize cargo market was essentially a duopoly of An-124 operators: Antonov Airlines and Volga-Dnepr. Since 2022 the two fleets have operated in largely separate markets, and one aircraft has famously sat parked at Toronto Pearson since February of that year. Demand for the type itself has never been higher.
Militaries and space programmes still book it months ahead: satellites, generators, helicopters, train locomotives. Nothing else in production carries what it carries.

aircraft.name: Boeing 787 Dreamliner
aircraft.headline: The composite revolution — the aircraft that made 200 new nonstop city pairs possible.
aircraft.identity: The aircraft that reinvented long-haul economics.
prose: The 787 was Boeing's bet that the future of long-haul wasn't bigger — it was leaner. Half the airframe by weight is carbon-fibre composite, the fuselage is wound as giant one-piece barrels, and the systems run on electricity instead of engine bleed air. The payoff: roughly 20% less fuel than the aircraft it replaced, a cabin pressurised lower and humidified higher, and windows a third larger that dim electronically.

Its real legacy is the route map. The 787 made thin, ultra-long routes profitable that no bigger jet could sustain — Perth to London, San Diego to Tokyo — opening hundreds of new nonstop city pairs and quietly killing the hub-only model the A380 was built for.

The programme survived a battery-fire grounding in 2013 and a bruising quality-control crisis in 2021–22. Over 1,200 have been delivered across three lengths; the 787-10 at 68.28 metres is the family's stretch and the 13th-longest aircraft ever built.

aircraft.name: Hughes H-4 Hercules
aircraft.headline: The Spruce Goose — the widest aircraft ever built until 2019, made of wood, flown for 26 seconds.
prose: Howard Hughes built the largest aircraft in the world out of birch, because wartime rules said he couldn't have aluminium. The H-4 was meant to carry 750 troops or a Sherman tank across the Atlantic, above the U-boats. The war ended before it was finished; the press mocked it as the Spruce Goose, a name Hughes hated for the rest of his life.

On 2 November 1947, during what was announced as a taxi test in Long Beach harbor, Hughes lifted it off the water himself — about 21 metres up, just over a kilometre, roughly 26 seconds. It never flew again. He kept it in climate-controlled secrecy, maintained by a full crew, until his death nearly thirty years later.

Its 97.54-metre wingspan remained the widest of any aircraft ever flown until the Stratolaunch carrier aircraft finally passed it in 2019 — seventy-two years later. The complete aircraft is preserved at the Evergreen Aviation Museum in Oregon.

aircraft.name: Tupolev Tu-144
aircraft.headline: The Soviet Concorde — first supersonic airliner to fly, and the race's tragic loser.
prose: The Tu-144 beat Concorde into the air by two months — 31 December 1968 — and past Mach 2 first as well. It was bigger and faster on paper, and needed retractable canard winglets behind the cockpit to land at manageable speeds. The Western press nicknamed it Concordski for its resemblance to its rival.

It won the race and lost everything after. One aircraft broke up in front of 250,000 people at the 1973 Paris Air Show; another crashed in testing in 1978. Its passenger career totalled 55 flights, Moscow to Almaty, cabin so loud that passengers passed notes — and then it was quietly withdrawn.

The strangest afterlife in aviation followed: NASA leased one in the 1990s as a flying supersonic laboratory. Sixteen were built; several survive in museums, including one improbably mounted on the roof of a technology museum in Sinsheim, Germany, next to a Concorde.

aircraft.name: Concorde
aircraft.headline: Mach 2 with a glass of champagne — the only supersonic airliner that ever really worked.
prose: Concorde cruised at Mach 2.04 — faster than a rifle bullet — at 60,000 feet, where passengers could see the curvature of the Earth. London to New York took under three and a half hours; the record was 2 hours 52 minutes. It flew faster than the sun moves: leave London at 10:30, land in New York at 9:20 the same morning.

It was an Anglo-French engineering triumph and a commercial failure — sonic booms banned it from land routes, the 1970s oil shock killed every order, and only the two national airlines ever flew the fourteen production aircraft. The airframe stretched 15–25 cm in flight from skin heating; the nose drooped for landing so pilots could see the runway.

The Paris crash of July 2000 broke the spell, and both fleets retired in October 2003. More than two decades on, nothing has replaced it: crossing the Atlantic takes twice as long today as it did in 1996. Every surviving Concorde is a museum piece, and they still draw the biggest crowds.

aircraft.name: Airbus A330
aircraft.headline: The quiet workhorse — over 1,800 built and still selling, thirty years on.
aircraft.identity: The world's most enduring widebody twin.
prose: The A330 has no single famous story — it just refuses to stop being the right answer. Born in 1992 as the twin-engine half of the A330/A340 pair, it watched its four-engine sibling die of fuel bills while it kept selling: to full-service flag carriers, budget long-haul startups, freight operators, and air forces (as the basis of the world's leading tanker, the A330 MRTT).

The A330neo re-engined it with the Trent 7000 and stretched the wings with sharklets, keeping it competitive against the 787 at a lower price. Over 1,850 of the family have been ordered across three decades — quietly one of the most successful widebodies ever.
Its cross-section also lives double lives: the BelugaXL outsize freighter is an A330 underneath, and hundreds of converted A330 freighters are coming as passenger examples age out.

aircraft.name: Airbus Beluga
aircraft.headline: The whale of the skies — Airbus's own aircraft for carrying pieces of other Airbuses.
prose: Airbus builds its aircraft across a continent — wings in Wales, fuselages in Germany, tails in Spain — and the Beluga is how the pieces meet in Toulouse and Hamburg. The original Beluga ST (an A300 with a whale's forehead) flew in 1994; the current BelugaXL, based on the A330, carries a full pair of A350 wings at once and wears a painted smile because Airbus staff voted for it.

The XL's cargo bay is the largest cross-section of any aircraft flying, loaded through a huge upward-hinging forward door above the dropped cockpit. Six were built, flying a constant shuttle between Airbus plants — statistically among the hardest-working aircraft in Europe.
The five older STs got a second career: Airbus Beluga Transport now hires them out for outsize cargo — satellites, helicopters, whatever won't fit in anything else except an An-124.

aircraft.name: McDonnell Douglas MD-11
aircraft.headline: The last great trijet — a passenger disappointment reborn as a freight legend.
prose: The MD-11 was the DC-10 perfected — stretched, wingletted, glass-cockpit, two crew instead of three, with the third engine still buried in the tail. It was also the aircraft that killed its maker: it missed its range guarantees at launch, airlines defected to the 777, and only 200 were built before Boeing absorbed McDonnell Douglas in 1997.

Then the second act. That capacious fuselage and huge payload made it a superb freighter, and for two decades the MD-11F was the backbone of FedEx, UPS and Lufthansa Cargo. Pilots respected it and feared it a little — small tail surfaces made it famously demanding to land.

The passenger version bowed out with KLM in 2014; the freighters are now on final approach too, with FedEx and UPS retirement dates set this decade. When the last one stops, the trijet era ends with it.

aircraft.name: Boeing 767
aircraft.headline: The transatlantic pioneer that never stopped — 45 years in production and counting.
aircraft.identity: The aircraft that opened the Atlantic to twins.
prose: The 767 changed the map more than almost any aircraft: it was the type that proved twin-engine jets could safely cross oceans, pioneering the ETOPS rules in the 1980s that every modern twin now flies under. Before the 767, crossing the Atlantic meant three or four engines; within a decade, it mostly meant a 767.

Its second act is freight. The passenger fleets are ageing out, but the 767-300F remains the world's default medium freighter — FedEx alone flies well over a hundred — and the line in Everett is still building them, plus the KC-46 Pegasus tanker for the US Air Force, more than four decades after first flight.
The stretched 767-400ER of 1999, at 61.37 metres with its raked wingtips, is the family's longest and rarest — only Delta and United ever bought it.

aircraft.name: Ilyushin Il-96
aircraft.headline: Russia's own widebody — the aircraft that carries the Kremlin.
aircraft.identity: The four-engine flagship of Russian aviation.
prose: The Il-96 is a four-engine, wingletted widebody that first flew in 1988. It arrived just as the airliner market was opening to worldwide competition, and only around 33 were completed.

Its most famous role is as Russia's presidential aircraft — the Il-96-300PU — with a small fleet operated by the Special Flight Detachment for state transport. Cubana flew it as the last airline operator.

Production has recently restarted with the stretched Il-96-400M, which at 63.94 metres is one of the longest aircraft ever built. Whether it returns to regular airline service is the open question for the type.

aircraft.name: Ilyushin Il-86
aircraft.headline: The Soviet Union's jumbo — 350 seats and stairs in its belly.
prose: The Il-86 was the Soviet Union's first widebody, and it solved a uniquely Soviet problem: airports without infrastructure. Passengers boarded up the aircraft's own built-in airstairs into the lower deck, hung their coats, stowed their own luggage on racks, then climbed internal staircases to the cabin — no jet bridges, no baggage handlers required.

It carried up to 350 people on trunk routes across the USSR for a quarter century, reliable and universally short on range — its engines were thirsty enough that Moscow to Havana needed multiple stops. Only 106 were built.
Airline service ended around 2011. A few airframes soldier on with the Russian military as the Il-80 'doomsday' airborne command posts — the Il-86's strangest legacy.

aircraft.name: Antonov An-22 Antei
aircraft.headline: The largest turboprop ever built — a giant that hauled tanks on propellers.
aircraft.identity: The largest turboprop aircraft in history.
prose: When the An-22 appeared at the 1965 Paris Air Show, unannounced, the West was stunned: a 250-tonne aircraft — on propellers. Four NK-12 engines, the most powerful turboprops ever made, each spinning two contra-rotating propellers, dragged 60-tonne payloads out of dirt strips no jet could use.

Named Antei after the giant of Greek myth, it set more than forty world records, carried tanks, missiles and drilling rigs across the Soviet Union, and pioneered the twin-tail, ramp-loading formula the An-124 and An-225 later perfected.
Sixty-eight were built. Antonov Airlines flew the world's last one commercially into the 2020s before retiring it — the end of the giant turboprop era.

aircraft.name: Douglas DC-8
aircraft.headline: The jet that broke the sound barrier — and outlived its rival by decades.
prose: The DC-8 lost the sales war to the 707 and won almost everything else. In August 1961 one was put into a shallow dive over Edwards and hit Mach 1.012 — the first airliner, and still one of very few, ever to go supersonic. And where 707s were largely scrap by the 1980s, DC-8s kept flying: the Super 60 stretches and later CFM56 re-engining made them durable freighters into the 2000s.
The Super 61 and 63, at 57.12 metres, were the longest airliners in the world until the 747 — a slim tube carrying 259 passengers, the true prototype of the stretched narrowbody.
NASA flew one as a flying laboratory until the 2020s, and Samaritan's Purse still operates a DC-8-72 on relief missions — a 1960s jet still earning its keep.

aircraft.name: North American XB-70 Valkyrie
aircraft.headline: Mach 3 on six engines — the bomber that rode its own shockwave.
prose: The XB-70 was designed to outrun anything: a strategic bomber cruising at Mach 3 at 70,000 feet, its six engines fed by a single vast intake, its wingtips folding down 65 degrees in flight to trap its own shockwave and ride it — compression lift, used by no other aircraft before or since.

Missiles made it obsolete before it flew; the programme was cut to two research aircraft. They flew beautifully and terribly: Valkyrie 2 was destroyed in 1966 when a chase plane was pulled into its wingtip vortex during a photo shoot, killing two pilots.

The survivor made its last flight in 1969 straight into the US Air Force Museum in Dayton, where it remains — 56.62 metres of white titanium and stainless steel, the fastest very large aircraft ever built.

aircraft.name: McDonnell Douglas DC-10
aircraft.headline: The trijet that survived its own reputation — and ended its days fighting fires.
prose: The DC-10 was born from the same 1966 airline requirement as the L-1011 and beat it by being cheaper and earlier. Then came the 1970s: cargo-door failures and the 1979 Chicago crash gave it the worst reputation in aviation. The design flaws were fixed, and the aircraft quietly became what the numbers always said it was — a rugged, useful widebody that sold 446 including sixty KC-10 tankers for the USAF.

It outlived its critics by decades. FedEx flew modernised MD-10s into the 2020s; Biman operated the last passenger flight in 2014; the KC-10 Extender refuelled American wars until its 2024 retirement.
Its final acts are its best: 10 Tanker's DC-10s drop 35,000 litres of retardant on wildfires in a single pass, and Orbis's MD-10 is a flying eye hospital with an operating theatre in the fuselage.

aircraft.name: Boeing 757
aircraft.headline: The narrowbody with widebody muscle — the aircraft airlines still can't replace.
prose: The 757 is the aircraft the industry has spent twenty years failing to replace. Overpowered engines and a big wing gave it a party trick no successor matches: full loads out of hot, high, short runways, and transatlantic range from a narrowbody — it's why thin routes like Newark to Edinburgh exist at all.

Production ended in 2004 at 1,050 aircraft, and the 'middle of the market' gap it left is an industry saga — the A321XLR is the first credible heir. Meanwhile Delta and United keep flying theirs hard, and huge freighter fleets at FedEx, UPS and DHL will carry parcels for years yet.
The 757-300 stretch, at 54.47 metres, is the longest single-aisle airliner ever built — a 243-seat pencil that only a few airlines dared order.

aircraft.name: Lockheed L-1011 TriStar
aircraft.headline: The over-engineered trijet — too good, too late, and the aircraft that ended Lockheed airliners.
aircraft.identity: The most advanced airliner of the 1970s.
prose: The TriStar was the engineer's trijet. It could autoland in zero visibility in 1972 — a capability so far ahead of its time that crews demonstrated coast-to-coast flights without touching the controls. Its third engine breathed through an elegant S-duct instead of the DC-10's straight-through tail, and pilots loved it without reservation.

It was also a business tragedy. Its Rolls-Royce engines bankrupted Rolls-Royce, the delay handed the market to the DC-10, and 250 aircraft weren't nearly enough: Lockheed lost billions and never built a civil airliner again.

One TriStar still flies — and it's the best one: Northrop Grumman's Stargazer, which climbs to 40,000 feet and drops Pegasus rockets that carry satellites to orbit. The most advanced airliner of its decade ended up as a spaceship's first stage.

aircraft.name: Tupolev Tu-160
aircraft.headline: The White Swan — the heaviest, fastest combat aircraft ever built, and the longest.
aircraft.identity: The largest combat aircraft in history.
prose: The Tu-160 is the biggest and heaviest combat aircraft ever flown: 275 tonnes at maximum weight, wings that sweep from 55.7 metres spread to 35.6 swept, and four afterburning engines that push it past Mach 2. Russian crews call it the White Swan for its anti-flash white finish; NATO calls it Blackjack.

It first flew in 1981 as the counterpart to the American B-1, entering service later that decade. Its variable-geometry wings sweep from 55.7 metres spread to 35.6 swept, and four afterburning NK-32s — the most powerful engines ever fitted to a combat aircraft — push it past Mach 2.

It is a rare giant of its generation still being built: production restarted with the modernised Tu-160M. At 54.1 metres it is the longest combat aircraft ever constructed, and the heaviest ever flown.

aircraft.name: Tupolev Tu-114
aircraft.headline: A bomber in an airline suit — still the fastest propeller airliner ever flown.
prose: The Tu-114 was a Tu-95 strategic bomber with an airliner fuselage — the fastest way for the USSR to get intercontinental range in 1957. It kept the bomber's swept wings and four monstrous NK-12 turboprops with contra-rotating propellers, and cruised at 770 km/h: a propeller speed record that has never been broken.

When Khrushchev flew it to New York in 1959, the aircraft was so tall no American airstairs could reach the door. It flew Moscow to Havana nonstop over 10,000 km, and in partnership with JAL carried the first Moscow–Tokyo services.

Only 32 were built, retired by 1976 — but its bomber parent flies on, and every droning Tu-95 that NATO intercepts today is powered by the same engines that made the Tu-114 the fastest propliner ever.

aircraft.name: Airbus A300
aircraft.headline: The aircraft that created Airbus — Europe's first widebody, now the freight world's favourite.
prose: Every Airbus traces back to this one. The A300 was the bet a new European consortium made in 1972: the world's first twin-engine widebody, at a time when Boeing said widebodies needed three or four engines. Early sales were so bad Airbus lent aircraft to Eastern Air Lines for free — then the fuel-efficient twin logic became the entire future of the industry.
The A300-600R matured it, and its cross-section proved perfect for containers: FedEx and UPS built enormous fleets, and the A300-600ST Beluga carried Airbus's own parts for 25 years.
Passenger A300s are nearly gone, but the freighters thunder on every night — over a hundred still working the parcel networks, half a century after the aircraft that started everything first flew.

aircraft.name: Bristol Brabazon
aircraft.headline: Britain's magnificent miscalculation — an ocean liner with wings that nobody ordered.
prose: The Brabazon was designed in wartime Britain for a post-war world that never arrived: a luxury liner flying London to New York with just 100 passengers in staterooms, a cinema, a cocktail bar and a promenade — each passenger allotted more space than a small flat. Its 70-metre wingspan exceeded a 747's; eight engines buried in the wing drove four pairs of contra-rotating propellers.

Bristol built a vast new assembly hall and demolished a village to extend the runway. The prototype flew beautifully in 1949 — and no airline wanted it. The economics of carrying 100 people in an aircraft that size were absurd next to smaller, faster types, and the coming jets made it prehistoric overnight.
It was scrapped in 1953 with under 400 hours flown. The consolation prize was real: the giant hall and runway at Filton later built Concorde.

aircraft.name: Ilyushin Il-62
aircraft.headline: The Soviet Union's long-range flagship — four engines on the tail and a stick to lean on.
prose: The Il-62 gave the Soviet Union its first true intercontinental jet — Moscow to Havana, New York and Tokyo — with all four engines clustered on the rear fuselage under a T-tail, the largest aircraft ever built in that configuration. So tail-heavy was the layout that it parked leaning on a deployable fourth leg under the tail.

It was engineered with remarkable simplicity — manual flight controls operated by aerodynamic tabs, no hydraulics needed — and served as the long-haul flagship for more than a dozen national carriers. 292 were built.
It has nearly vanished: North Korea's Air Koryo keeps the last passenger examples airworthy, alongside a couple of Russian state machines — flying museum pieces from 1963 still crossing continents.

aircraft.name: Boeing C-17 Globemaster III
aircraft.headline: The airlifter that lands anywhere — a strategic giant with tactical manners.
prose: The C-17's genius is contradiction: it hauls strategic loads — a 60-tonne main battle tank — yet lands on 1,000-metre dirt strips, backs up under its own reverse thrust, and turns around on a runway the width of a road. Nothing else combines intercontinental reach with rough-field manners at this scale.

It became the machine the world reaches for when everything goes wrong: earthquake relief, evacuation airlifts, aid drops. The 2021 Kabul evacuation made one C-17 famous for carrying 823 people in a single lift.

279 were built before the line closed in 2015 — a decision the US has publicly regretted since, as no replacement exists. Nine air forces fly it, and every operator's answer to 'what would you buy more of?' is the same.

aircraft.name: Vickers VC10
aircraft.headline: Britain's elegant long-hauler — still the fastest subsonic Atlantic crossing by a jetliner.
aircraft.identity: The fastest subsonic airliner across the Atlantic.
prose: The VC10 was designed for the Empire's leftovers: hot, high, short colonial runways that the 707 couldn't use. Four Conways on the tail left a huge clean wing, giving it superb short-field performance and a hushed cabin — BOAC sold it as 'swift and silent', and passengers preferred it to anything American.

It still holds a record that matters: the fastest subsonic jet airliner crossing of the Atlantic — 5 hours 1 minute, New York to London, set in 1979 and never beaten by anything without afterburners except Concorde.

Only 54 were built; the economics never matched the elegance. The RAF gave it a 47-year second life as a tanker, retiring the last one in 2013 — the final flight of a British-built long-haul jetliner.

aircraft.name: Boeing B-52 Stratofortress
aircraft.headline: The bomber that will outlive its designers, its pilots' parents, and possibly a full century.
prose: The B-52 first flew in 1952, and the US Air Force currently plans to fly it into the 2050s — a service life of a full century, unmatched by any combat aircraft in history. Crews today fly the same airframes their grandfathers flew; the last one was built in 1962.

Eight engines hang in four twin pods under a drooping 56.4-metre wing so flexible the outriggers at its tips keep it off the tarmac. Across seven decades it has been continually re-equipped and re-tasked — few airframes in history have been reinvented so many times.

76 B-52Hs remain, and rather than retire them the USAF is fitting new Rolls-Royce F130 engines and new radars to create the B-52J. The joke in Air Force circles is that the last B-52 pilot has not been born yet — and it may not be a joke.

aircraft.name: Ilyushin Il-76
aircraft.headline: The world's everywhere-freighter — nearly a thousand built and still rolling off the line.
aircraft.identity: The most-produced heavy airlifter in history.
prose: No heavy transport has been built in greater numbers: over 960 Il-76s since 1971, and modernised Il-76MD-90As are still being produced today. It was designed to feed Siberia — 50 tonnes into unpaved strips in any weather — with a glazed navigator's nose out of a 1950s bomber and landing gear that laughs at gravel.

It became the developing world's airlifter, the UN's aid truck, a water bomber, a hospital, a zero-G trainer, the platform for Russia's A-50 radar aircraft and even a flying laser testbed. If something heavy landed somewhere improbable in the last fifty years, an Il-76 probably brought it.

China builds its own derivative lineage from it (the Y-20 owes it much), India flies a fleet, and its distinctive T-tail silhouette and 46.59-metre length close out our list of the fifty longest aircraft ever built.

aircraft.name: Airbus A320 family
aircraft.headline: The fly-by-wire revolution — and the aircraft that finally caught the 737.
prose: The A320 did something no airliner had done before: it handed the controls to a computer. Fly-by-wire with flight-envelope protection, sidesticks instead of yokes, and a glass cockpit — in 1987, when the competition still flew cables and pulleys. Every Airbus since has been built on that architecture, and pilots can cross-qualify across the whole family because of it.

It also won the numbers war. The A320 family overtook the 737 in cumulative orders, and the A321neo — 44.51 metres long, transatlantic-capable in XLR form — has become the aircraft that reshaped medium-haul flying, opening routes a narrowbody had no business flying.
Almost 12,000 have been delivered across the family, and the backlog runs years deep. If you have flown in the last decade, the odds are very good it was on one of these.

aircraft.name: Airbus A220
aircraft.headline: The clean-sheet small jet Bombardier built, and a trade war handed to Airbus.
aircraft.identity: The best small airliner of its generation.
prose: The A220 began life as the Bombardier CSeries: a clean-sheet design with geared turbofans, a composite wing, and a cabin so quiet and roomy that passengers noticed. It was the right aircraft — and it nearly destroyed its maker, because the development cost was ruinous and a US trade dispute threatened crushing tariffs.

Airbus took a majority stake for one dollar in 2018, moved production out of tariff reach, and renamed it. The aircraft that almost bankrupted Bombardier is now one of Airbus's most in-demand products.
It fills the gap below the A320 with genuine long legs — the A220-300 can fly 6,297 km, enough to cross the Atlantic on thin routes that a bigger jet could never fill.

aircraft.name: Embraer E-Jet E2
aircraft.headline: Brazil's answer to the big two — the most efficient small jet flying.
aircraft.identity: The regional jet that grew up.
prose: Embraer built the third-largest commercial aircraft business in the world by owning a market Boeing and Airbus did not want: jets for 70 to 140 people, on routes too thin for a 737. The original E-Jets sold over 1,700; the E2 generation re-engined them with geared turbofans and a new wing.

The E195-E2 is the biggest of them — 41.5 metres long, carrying up to 146 people, and burning notably less fuel per seat than anything in its class. Airlines that once flew it as a feeder now fly it on real routes.

Boeing tried to buy the commercial division outright in 2019. The deal collapsed during the pandemic, and Embraer stayed independent — which, given how the last decade went for Boeing, looks like a lucky escape.

aircraft.name: COMAC C919
aircraft.headline: China's bid to break the Airbus–Boeing duopoly.
prose: For fifty years, a large airliner meant Boeing or Airbus. The C919 is China's attempt to make that a three-horse race: a 164-seat narrowbody aimed squarely at the 737 and the A320, first flown in 2017 and carrying fare-paying passengers since 2023.

How independent it really is remains the central question — the LEAP-1C engines, avionics and many systems are Western, and export sanctions are a standing risk to the programme. Chinese suppliers are working to replace them.

Production is still measured in dozens, not hundreds, and it holds no Western certification, so it flies domestic Chinese routes. But with the world's largest aviation market as a captive customer, it does not need anyone else's permission to matter.

aircraft.name: Boeing 727
aircraft.headline: Three engines, built-in stairs, and the jet that could land anywhere.
aircraft.identity: The jet that opened the small airports.
prose: The 727 was designed to bring jets to airports that could not handle them: short runways, no ground equipment, thin markets. Boeing gave it a remarkable high-lift wing with triple-slotted flaps, three engines clustered at the tail so the wing stayed clean, and airstairs built into the rear so it could turn around anywhere.
It worked so well that it became the first jetliner to pass 1,000 sales, and 1,832 were built — a record that stood until the 737 passed it. For two decades it was the sound of American aviation.

Noise rules and fuel prices retired it from the airlines, and the famous rear airstair was locked shut after D.B. Cooper parachuted out of one in 1971. A handful still fly as freighters and government aircraft.

aircraft.name: De Havilland Canada Dash 8-400
aircraft.headline: The turboprop that flies like a jet — and lands where jets cannot.
aircraft.identity: The fastest turboprop airliner in service.
prose: The Dash 8-400 — sold for years as the Q400, the Q standing for Quiet — closed the gap between the turboprop and the jet. It cruises at 667 km/h, faster than any other turboprop airliner and close enough to a regional jet that passengers rarely notice the difference, while burning roughly a third less fuel.

Its party trick is the runway. Big propellers and a high wing let it work short, steep and awkward airfields that regional jets simply cannot use, which is why it became the signature aircraft of operators flying into difficult terrain and small city airports.

It was Bombardier's, then De Havilland Canada's again after the 2019 sale, and it is the aircraft most people picture when they think of a modern turboprop: high wing, T-tail, and two enormous six-bladed propellers.

aircraft.name: ATR 72
aircraft.headline: The most successful regional turboprop of the modern era.
prose: ATR is a Franco-Italian joint venture that has quietly won the regional turboprop market outright: over 1,200 ATR 72s built, flying for more than 100 operators, on routes where a jet would burn twice the fuel for a journey barely any quicker.

It is slower than a Dash 8 and cheaper to run than almost anything, and on the short island- and island-hopping legs it was designed for, that trade is exactly right. Under an hour in the air, and fuel burn is the only number that matters.

Its high wing, slim fuselage and huge six-bladed propellers make it one of the most recognisable aircraft in regional aviation — and one of the last places you can still watch propellers do serious commercial work.

An all-widebody airline: nothing smaller than a Boeing 777 flies in Emirates colours.
Every seat in the fleet has a screen — its ice entertainment system has topped the awards tables for two decades.

Was once the world's largest 747 operator; the last of its jumbos retired in 2020.

metrics.name: Seats per metre
metrics.why: A density proxy you can compare across sizes. It is how the A380's double deck shows up in a single number — more seats per metre of fuselage than anything else flying.

metrics.name: Range per tonne
metrics.why: How far each tonne of maximum weight takes you. Smaller aircraft score dramatically higher — it is an honest way to see why the industry moved from quads to light twins.

metrics.name: Span-to-length ratio
metrics.why: A shape signature. Above 1.0 the wing is wider than the aircraft is long — rare, and the A380 is the only one here that does it.

methodology.caveats: Derived metrics use each family's flagship variant as specified in its table. Typical seating is the manufacturer's multi-class figure; real airline layouts vary. These are comparison tools, not operational data.

makers.name: Airbus
makers.tagline: The European consortium that took half the world's airliner market.
prose: Airbus began in 1970 as a political improbability: a consortium of French, German, British and Spanish partners pooling their aircraft industries to challenge American dominance. Its first product, the A300, was the world's first twin-engine widebody — an aircraft the established manufacturers said the market did not want.

Two decades later Airbus had pioneered fly-by-wire in airliners with the A320, and by the 2000s it was building the largest passenger aircraft ever flown. Today it splits the world's airliner market with Boeing almost evenly, and its aircraft on this site range from the A300 that started it all to the A380 and the composite A350.

makers.name: Boeing
makers.tagline: A century of aircraft — and the two most-produced airliner families in history.
prose: Boeing has been building aircraft since 1916, and for most of the jet age it defined what an airliner was. The 707 made intercontinental jet travel routine, the 747 made it affordable, and the 737 became the most-produced jet airliner in history by a distance that no rival has approached.

Its range on this site is unmatched: the 737 and 757 narrowbodies, the 767 and 777 twins, the composite 787, the 747 and its Dreamlifter derivative, the 777X now in certification — and the B-52, an aircraft first flown in 1952 that its air force plans to keep flying into the 2050s.

makers.name: Antonov
makers.tagline: The design bureau that built the largest aircraft the world has ever seen.
prose: No manufacturer owns the very top of the size charts like Antonov. Founded in 1946 as a Soviet design bureau and headquartered in Kyiv, USSR, it specialised in heavy lift: rugged, ramp-loading transports that could take enormous payloads out of unpaved strips.

Its lineage climbs relentlessly. The An-22 of 1965 remains the largest turboprop ever built. The An-124 became the largest production cargo aircraft in the world. And the An-225, built as a single aircraft to carry a space shuttle, was the longest and heaviest aircraft ever flown — records that still stand today.

makers.name: Lockheed
makers.tagline: Military giants, one beautiful trijet, and the fastest aircraft ever built.
prose: Lockheed's reputation rests on doing what others could not: the U-2, the SR-71 Blackbird, and — in the heavy-lift world — the C-5 Galaxy, which for decades was the largest aircraft in the Western world and can still swallow loads nothing else in NATO can carry.

Its one great airliner was the L-1011 TriStar: technically the most advanced jet of its decade, capable of automatic landings in 1972, and a commercial disaster that ended Lockheed's civil aircraft business for good. The company merged with Martin Marietta in 1995 to become Lockheed Martin.

makers.name: Douglas & McDonnell Douglas
makers.tagline: From the DC-3 to the last great trijets — the manufacturer Boeing absorbed.
prose: Douglas Aircraft once ruled the skies: the DC-3 made air travel a business, and the DC-8 took it into the jet age, becoming the only airliner in history to exceed the speed of sound in a dive. Merged with McDonnell in 1967, the combined company built the DC-10 and the MD-11 — the last three-engine widebodies ever produced.

The MD-11's commercial failure, alongside a shrinking military portfolio, ended the company's independence: Boeing acquired McDonnell Douglas in 1997. Its aircraft fly on mostly as freighters, and its trijets remain among the most distinctive silhouettes ever built.

makers.name: Ilyushin
makers.tagline: Heavy transports and long-haul airliners built to work anywhere.
prose: Ilyushin's aircraft were designed for a country of vast distances and rough airfields, and it shows in every one of them: robust landing gear, high wings, ramps, and a tolerance for conditions that would ground more delicate machines.

The Il-62 gave the Soviet Union its first intercontinental jet, the Il-86 its first widebody, and the Il-96 its four-engine flagship. The Il-76, first flown in 1971 and still in production today, is the most-produced heavy airlifter in history — the workhorse that shows up wherever something big has to be moved.

makers.name: Tupolev
makers.tagline: Bombers, airliners, and the first supersonic transport ever to fly.
prose: Tupolev has always blurred the line between bomber and airliner — its Tu-114, still the fastest propeller-driven airliner ever built, was a strategic bomber with a passenger fuselage grafted on, and it flew at 770 km/h on contra-rotating propellers.

The bureau got the Tu-144 into the air two months before Concorde, making it the first supersonic transport ever to fly. Its Tu-160 is the largest and heaviest combat aircraft ever built, and remains in production in modernised form.

makers.name: Aérospatiale / BAC
makers.tagline: Two national industries, one treaty, and the only supersonic airliner that worked.
prose: Concorde was not built by a company — it was built by a treaty. In 1962 Britain and France signed an agreement so binding that neither government could withdraw without paying the other's costs, which is the only reason the aircraft survived the decade.

Aérospatiale in Toulouse and the British Aircraft Corporation in Filton built it together, sharing a production line 800 kilometres apart. Both companies were later folded into what became Airbus and BAE Systems, but Concorde remains their monument: the only supersonic airliner ever to fly a sustained commercial career.

makers.name: Hughes
makers.tagline: One billionaire, one wooden giant, one 26-second flight.
prose: Howard Hughes built aircraft the way he did everything else — enormously, secretively, and on his own terms. His H-4 Hercules was the largest aircraft in the world, built from laminated birch because wartime rules reserved aluminium for other programmes.

It flew once, for about 26 seconds, in November 1947, with Hughes himself at the controls. Its 97.54-metre wingspan stood as the widest of any aircraft ever flown for seventy-two years — a record broken only in 2019.

makers.name: North American Aviation
makers.tagline: The Mustang, the X-15, and a Mach 3 bomber made of steel.
prose: North American built some of the most extraordinary aircraft of the twentieth century: the P-51 Mustang, the F-86 Sabre, and the X-15 rocket plane that still holds the speed record for a crewed aircraft.

Its XB-70 Valkyrie was the most ambitious of all — a 56-metre bomber cruising at Mach 3, its wingtips folding down in flight to ride its own shockwave. Two were built, one survives, and nothing that large has ever flown that fast since.

sources.name: National Museum of the USAF — XB-70

makers.name: Bristol Aeroplane Company
makers.tagline: The Brabazon — Britain's magnificent, unwanted giant.
prose: Bristol built aircraft and engines at Filton for most of the twentieth century, but its most famous project was also its greatest miscalculation: the Brabazon, a 70-metre-span luxury airliner designed to carry 100 passengers across the Atlantic in ocean-liner comfort.
No airline ever ordered it, and the prototype was scrapped in 1953. The vast assembly hall and extended runway built for it were not wasted, though — a decade later, they built Concorde.

makers.name: Vickers
makers.tagline: The VC10 — elegant, over-engineered, and still holding a record.
prose: Vickers-Armstrongs built the Viscount, the world's first turboprop airliner, and then the VC10: four engines on the tail, a huge clean wing, and short-field performance designed for hot, high runways that American jets could not use.

Only 54 were built and it never made money, but it still holds the record for the fastest subsonic crossing of the Atlantic by a jet airliner — five hours and one minute, set in 1979 and never beaten. Vickers became part of the British Aircraft Corporation in 1960.

makers.name: Embraer
makers.tagline: The third-largest commercial aircraft maker in the world.
prose: Embraer was founded as a Brazilian state project in 1969 and privatised in 1994, and it grew by taking a market the giants ignored: jets carrying 70 to 150 people. Its E-Jets sold more than 1,700 and became the backbone of regional flying across the Americas and Europe.

The E2 generation added geared turbofans and a new wing, making it one of the most fuel-efficient small jets in the sky. Boeing agreed to buy the commercial division in 2019, then walked away during the pandemic — leaving Embraer independent, and still the only serious competitor to the duopoly below 150 seats.

makers.name: COMAC
makers.tagline: The state-backed challenger to the Airbus–Boeing duopoly.
prose: The Commercial Aircraft Corporation of China was created in 2008 with a single strategic goal: end China's dependence on Boeing and Airbus. It is state-owned, state-funded, and working to a timescale measured in decades rather than quarters.

Its C919 narrowbody entered service in 2023 and is now flying paying passengers on Chinese domestic routes. Production is still small and many key systems are Western-supplied, but with the world's largest aviation market at home, COMAC is the first credible third player in fifty years.

makers.name: De Havilland Canada
makers.tagline: Bush planes, short fields, and the fastest turboprop airliner flying.
prose: De Havilland Canada built its reputation on aircraft that could operate where nothing else could — the Beaver, the Otter, the Twin Otter — rugged short-field designs that opened up the Canadian north and are still working around the world decades later.

The Dash 8 carried that philosophy into the airline world, and the Dash 8-400 remains the fastest turboprop airliner in service. The company was absorbed into Bombardier, then re-established under its own name in 2019 — a rare case of an aviation marque coming back from the dead.

makers.name: ATR
makers.tagline: The Franco-Italian venture that owns the regional turboprop market.
prose: ATR — Avions de Transport Régional — is a joint venture between Airbus and Leonardo, founded in 1981 to build regional turboprops. It has done exactly one thing since, and done it better than anyone: over 1,700 aircraft delivered across the ATR 42 and ATR 72.

On short regional legs, its economics are almost impossible to argue with: roughly half the fuel of a comparable regional jet for a journey only marginally slower. It is the reason propellers are still serious business in commercial aviation.

types.name: Widebody airliners
types.tagline: Two aisles, two decks sometimes, and the backbone of long-haul travel.
prose: A widebody is any airliner with two aisles — a fuselage wide enough that passengers walk down two corridors instead of one, typically 5 to 6.5 metres across. The format was invented by the Boeing 747 and the Airbus A300 around 1970, and it made mass long-haul travel possible.

Widebodies dominate the record boards for a simple reason: length and wingspan scale with the payload they carry across oceans. Every aircraft here carries a two-aisle cabin, and between them they fly the great majority of the world's intercontinental routes.

types.name: Narrowbody airliners
types.tagline: One aisle, and the aircraft you actually fly on most.
prose: A narrowbody has a single aisle — a fuselage roughly 3 to 4 metres wide. They are the workhorses of aviation: the vast majority of flights taken worldwide every day are on single-aisle aircraft, and the 737 alone has been built more than any other jet airliner in history.

They rarely top the size charts, but the stretched ones sneak on: the Boeing 757-300, at 54.47 metres, is the longest single-aisle airliner ever built — a remarkably thin pencil of an aircraft carrying up to 243 people.

types.name: Freighters & outsize cargo
types.tagline: The aircraft built to swallow what nothing else can carry.
prose: Cargo aircraft are shaped by their doors. A nose that hinges upward, a ramp at the back, a fuselage that is essentially a tube of empty volume — every design decision follows from the need to get enormous objects in and out quickly.

This is where aviation's true giants live. Outsize freighters like the An-124 and the An-225 exist to carry generators, turbines, satellites and locomotives — payloads for which there is simply no alternative. The Beluga and Dreamlifter are stranger still: aircraft built to carry the pieces of other aircraft.

types.name: Military transports
types.tagline: Strategic reach, tactical manners, and a tank in the hold.
prose: A military airlifter has to do two contradictory things: fly intercontinental distances with a strategic payload, then land on a short, unpaved strip and unload without ground equipment. High wings, ramps, and enormous multi-wheel landing gear are the standard answers.

The C-5 Galaxy and the C-17 Globemaster III between them define Western heavy lift, and both are regularly the first aircraft into a disaster zone. They are the reason a 60-tonne tank or an entire field hospital can cross the world overnight.

types.name: Bombers & experimental giants
types.tagline: The largest and fastest combat aircraft ever built.
prose: Strategic bombers push airframe design harder than almost anything else: intercontinental range, heavy loads, and — in some eras — extreme speed. Two of them appear on our records board purely by size.

The Tu-160 is the longest and heaviest combat aircraft ever constructed. The B-52 has been flying since 1952 and is planned to serve into the 2050s. And the XB-70 Valkyrie, an experimental Mach 3 bomber, remains the fastest very large aircraft ever flown.

types.name: Supersonic transports
types.tagline: Two aircraft, one era, and a speed record nobody has taken back.
prose: Only two supersonic airliners have ever carried fare-paying passengers: Concorde and the Tupolev Tu-144. Both were slender deltas with afterburning engines, both flew at roughly twice the speed of sound, and both were commercial failures.
Concorde crossed the Atlantic in under three hours and retired in 2003. More than two decades later, nothing has replaced it — the same journey takes twice as long today as it did in 1996.

types.name: Trijets
types.tagline: Three engines: the answer to a rule that no longer exists.
prose: The trijet existed because of regulation. Before modern ETOPS rules, twin-engine aircraft were barred from flying far from a diversion airport, so an aircraft crossing an ocean needed three engines or four. Three was cheaper than four — hence the DC-10, the L-1011 and later the MD-11, each with an engine buried in the tail.

Once twins were certified for long overwater flights, the economics collapsed instantly: two engines burn less fuel than three. No trijet airliner has been built since 2000, and the last freighters are now retiring.

types.name: Propliners & flying boats
types.tagline: The giants of the propeller age — and the turboprops still working today.
prose: The propeller era produced aircraft of a scale that still surprises people. Piston and turboprop giants carried the ambitions of their age: the Bristol Brabazon's ocean-liner luxury, the Hughes H-4's 97.54-metre wingspan, and the Tu-114's 770 km/h — still the fastest a propeller airliner has ever flown.

The giants are gone, but propellers never left. On short regional routes the turboprop is simply the better answer: the ATR 72 and the Dash 8-400 burn roughly half the fuel of a comparable jet for a journey barely any slower, and they work short, steep runways that jets cannot touch. Over 1,800 of the two are flying today.

explained.name: MTOW — maximum take-off weight
explained.tagline: The single number that decides what an aircraft can actually do.
prose: MTOW is the greatest weight at which an aircraft is certified to begin its take-off roll: the airframe, the fuel, the passengers, the cargo — everything, all at once. It is not a measure of how much the aircraft weighs. It is a measure of how much it can lift.

It matters because almost every other limit follows from it. Fuel and payload compete for the same allowance, which is why a fully loaded aircraft cannot also carry full tanks. It sets the runway length required, the landing fees an airline pays, and which airports the aircraft can use at all.

It is also the honest way to rank aircraft by size. Length flatters slim aircraft and wingspan flatters gliding efficiency, but MTOW measures raw lifting power — which is why the An-225, at 640 tonnes, sits alone at the top of the board.

explained.name: ETOPS — how twins were allowed over oceans
explained.tagline: The rule change that killed the four-engine airliner.
prose: ETOPS stands for Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards, and it answers one question: how far from a diversion airport may a two-engine aircraft fly? Before it existed, the answer was 60 minutes — which effectively barred twins from crossing oceans and made three or four engines mandatory for long-haul.

As engines became dramatically more reliable, regulators extended the limit: 120 minutes, then 180, then beyond. An ETOPS-330 aircraft may fly up to five and a half hours from the nearest suitable runway on one engine. The Boeing 767 pioneered the regime in the 1980s; the 777 and A350 perfected it.

The consequence reshaped aviation. Two engines burn less fuel than four, so once twins were allowed everywhere, the four-engine airliner became economically indefensible. The A340, the 747 and ultimately the A380 were all killed by a rule change, not by a better aircraft. Pilots have a grim joke about the acronym: Engines Turn Or Passengers Swim.

explained.name: Range versus payload — the trade nobody advertises
explained.tagline: An aircraft cannot carry a full load as far as it can fly.
prose: Every quoted range figure hides an assumption about how much the aircraft is carrying, because fuel and payload draw on the same weight allowance. Fill the seats and the hold, and there is less allowance left for fuel — so the aircraft cannot fly as far. Fill the tanks instead, and something has to come out of the cabin.

Manufacturers describe this with a payload-range diagram: a chart whose line falls away as range increases. Airlines live on that curve. It is why a route can be flown eastbound but not westbound against the jet stream, why some long flights block out the last rows of seats, and why an aircraft advertised at 15,000 km may only manage that with a modest load.
So when this site quotes a range, it quotes the manufacturer's figure at a stated payload, and says which one. A range number without a payload attached is marketing, not engineering.

explained.name: Winglets, sharklets and raked tips
explained.tagline: The bent-up bits at the end of the wing, and why they are worth millions.
prose: A wing generates lift by holding higher pressure underneath than above — and at the wingtip, that pressure difference escapes around the end, spiralling off as a vortex. That vortex is pure waste: it is drag, it is noise, and it is fuel burned for nothing.

Wingtip devices interrupt it. A vertical winglet, an Airbus sharklet, or a Boeing raked tip each spreads the escaping air over a longer path, cutting induced drag by a few per cent. A few per cent of an airline's fuel bill is an enormous amount of money, which is why they appeared on almost everything after the 1990s.

There is a second, sneakier reason. Airports allocate gates by wingspan category, so a wing cannot simply be made longer without pushing the aircraft into a bigger, costlier gate class. A device that curves upward buys aerodynamic span without buying physical width — which is exactly why the 777X's wingtips fold.

explained.name: The high-bypass turbofan
explained.tagline: Why modern engines are enormous fans with a small jet buried inside.
prose: A pure turbojet throws a small amount of air backwards very fast. A high-bypass turbofan does the opposite: it throws an enormous amount of air backwards fairly slowly, and that turns out to be far more efficient for the speeds airliners actually fly.

Most of the air entering the huge fan at the front never touches the combustion chamber at all — it bypasses the core and goes straight out of the back. On a modern engine, eight or more times as much air bypasses the core as passes through it. The buried jet engine exists mainly to spin the fan.

This is why engines have grown so visibly fat: the fan of a GE9X on the 777X is roughly as wide as the entire fuselage of a Boeing 737. Bigger fan, more bypass, less fuel — and dramatically less noise, because a slow, wide column of air is far quieter than a thin, fast one.

explained.name: Widebody versus narrowbody
explained.tagline: One aisle or two — the division that organises the whole industry.
prose: A narrowbody has a single aisle and a fuselage roughly three to four metres across. A widebody has two aisles and a fuselage of five to six and a half metres. That one difference cascades into almost everything else: how many people fit, how fast the aircraft can be boarded and turned around, whether standard cargo containers fit underfloor, and how far it makes economic sense to fly.

Widebodies exist to cross oceans with enough people to justify the trip. Narrowbodies exist to fly short and often — and they are what most passengers actually experience, because the vast majority of flights taken worldwide every day are single-aisle.

The line between them is blurring. The A321XLR is a narrowbody with transatlantic range, flying routes that once demanded a widebody. It is the most interesting fight in commercial aviation right now.

posts.title: Why the four-engine airliner is extinct
posts.dek: The 747, the A340 and the A380 were not beaten by a better aeroplane. They were beaten by a rule change, and by an engine that stopped breaking.
posts.author: aircraft.fyi

body.p: Walk through any major airport today and you will struggle to find an airliner with four engines. Twenty years ago they were the aristocracy of the sky: the 747 on every prestige route, the A340 crossing oceans for Lufthansa and Cathay, and then the A380, the largest passenger aircraft ever built, arriving in 2007 to a genuine sense of occasion.

All three are finished. Airbus closed the A380 line after 251 aircraft. The A340 sold 377 and stopped. Boeing built the last passenger 747 in 2017 and the last freighter in 2023, ending a fifty-four-year production run. Nothing is being designed to replace any of them.

The obvious explanation is that a better four-engine aircraft came along. It did not. What happened is far more interesting: the four-engine airliner was killed by a regulation, and the regulation only changed because engines stopped failing.

body.h2: Why four engines existed at all

body.p: Not for power. Four engines existed because of a rule called ETOPS — Extended-range Twin-engine Operational Performance Standards — and specifically because of what preceded it. Until the 1980s, a twin-engine airliner could not fly more than sixty minutes from a suitable diversion airport. Look at a map of the North Atlantic and you will see the problem immediately: a sixty-minute rule makes the crossing essentially impossible for a twin.

So if you wanted to fly the Atlantic or the Pacific, you needed three engines or four. That is the entire reason the trijet existed — the DC-10, the L-1011, the 727 — and it is why the 747 had four. The engineering did not demand it. The regulation did.

Through the 1970s and 1980s, jet engine reliability improved by an order of magnitude. In-flight shutdown rates fell so far that the statistical case for the sixty-minute rule collapsed. Regulators responded by extending the limit: 120 minutes, then 180, then 207, then 330. An ETOPS-330 twin may fly five and a half hours from the nearest runway on one engine.

The Boeing 767 was the aircraft that proved it, opening transatlantic twin operations in the 1980s. The 777 industrialised it. The 787 and A350 made it routine. And once a twin could fly anywhere a quad could, the comparison became purely economic — and the economics are not close.

Two large engines are cheaper than four smaller ones producing the same total thrust. Fewer parts, fewer overhauls, fewer spares, fewer hours of maintenance per flight, and — because engine efficiency improves with size — less fuel per unit of thrust. A modern high-bypass turbofan the size of a 777X's GE9X has a fan roughly as wide as a 737's fuselage, and it is dramatically more efficient than a 1990s engine a third of its size.
Look at what our own data says about the aircraft that replaced each other.

The 777-300ER carries almost as many people almost as far as a 747-8, on two engines instead of four, at a hundred tonnes less weight. The A350-1000 flies further than either of them and weighs less than both. There is no spec-sheet argument left for the quad. There is only nostalgia, and nostalgia does not pay the fuel bill.

body.h2: So why did the A380 happen at all?

body.p: Because Airbus was betting on a different future. The A380 assumed hub-and-spoke: passengers funnelled into a handful of enormous airports and moved between them in enormous aeroplanes. On that assumption it made sense — on a slot-constrained route like Heathrow to Dubai, an aircraft carrying over five hundred people per slot is a very powerful thing, which is exactly why Emirates ordered 116 of them and is the only airline that ever truly made it work.

body.callout: The A380 was not a bad aircraft. It was a correct answer to a question the industry stopped asking.

body.p: What actually happened was point-to-point. Efficient long-range twins made thin, direct routes profitable — Manchester to Austin rather than Manchester to Heathrow to Austin. Passengers preferred it, airlines preferred it, and the mega-hub logic quietly deflated. The A380 needed the world to consolidate. The world dispersed instead.

Four engines survive where the rules do not apply and the economics are not the point: on outsize freighters like the An-124, on the C-5 Galaxy, on the B-52 which will fly with eight into the 2050s. Military aviation is not required to make money.

For everyone else, the last four-engine passenger aircraft in the world are already in their final decade of service. The A380s at Emirates will fly into the 2030s, and then the shape of them — that impossible double-decker in the landing lights — simply will not be in the sky any more.
It was not killed by a rival. It was killed by a paperwork change and by an engine that refused to break.

posts.title: Is supersonic travel actually coming back?
posts.dek: Boom Supersonic has flown a demonstrator, built a factory and taken 130 orders. It still has not run its engine. Here is where the comeback really stands in mid-2026 — and how Overture measures up against Concorde.
posts.author: aircraft.fyi

body.p: Concorde flew its last commercial service on 24 October 2003. For the twenty-three years since, the fastest way to cross the Atlantic has been slower than it was in 1996 — an almost unique reversal in the history of transport. Aviation is the only major mode of travel that got slower.

Boom Supersonic, founded in Denver in 2014, exists to undo that. And unlike the parade of supersonic startups that produced nothing but renders, Boom has now done something undeniably real: on 28 January 2025, its XB-1 demonstrator broke the sound barrier over the Mojave, reaching Mach 1.122 at 35,290 feet. It was the first independently developed supersonic jet in history, and the first civil supersonic aircraft built in America ever to go supersonic.
That is a genuine milestone and it deserves credit. It is also, in the brutal arithmetic of aircraft certification, the easy part. Here is where the comeback actually stands.

Overture is designed to cruise at Mach 1.7 at up to 60,000 feet, carrying 64 to 80 passengers in an all-business-class cabin, with a range of 4,250 nautical miles — roughly 7,870 km. It is a tailed delta with four medium-bypass, non-afterburning engines slung in external pods, built largely from carbon composite, and designed to run on 100% sustainable aviation fuel.
The most striking thing about it, if you put the numbers next to Concorde, is how familiar it looks.

Overture is almost exactly Concorde's length. It is slower, carries fewer people, and flies slightly further. That is not a failure of ambition — it is the entire strategy. Concorde's Mach 2.04 forced it into exotic materials, afterburners and a fuel burn that only made sense with first-class fares on a handful of routes. Dropping to Mach 1.7 keeps the airframe in aluminium-and-composite temperature territory, allows conventional non-afterburning engines, and — Boom argues — brings the economics down to something an airline can sell as business class.

body.h2: The catch nobody can engineer around: the engine

body.p: When Boom went looking for an engine, Rolls-Royce, GE, Pratt & Whitney and Safran all declined to build one. The capital cost of a clean-sheet supersonic engine, for a market of maybe a few hundred aircraft, did not add up for any of them. So in December 2022 Boom announced it would build its own — Symphony, a 40,000-lbf medium-bypass turbofan.

This is the crux of the entire programme, and it is worth being precise about where it stands. Symphony passed a key technical review in March 2026 that released it into manufacturing. Boom says roughly 95% of the parts for the first prototype core are in production. The core — a 12-foot, 4-foot-diameter high-pressure spool of compressor, combustor and turbine — is due to be assembled around mid-2026 and then run at Boom's own test site at the Colorado Air & Space Port, with the validation campaign later in 2026.

body.callout: As of mid-2026, the engine that Overture depends on has not yet completed a full core test run.

body.p: No aerospace startup has ever certified a clean-sheet airframe and a bespoke engine simultaneously. Boeing and Airbus, with vastly greater resources and institutional experience, routinely take a decade to certify one or the other. Boom's public target remains entry into service in 2029 — CEO Blake Scholl reaffirmed it at the AIAA Aviation Forum on 11 June 2026. Independent analysts, including Forecast International, expect the date to slip into the 2030s. Both things can be true: the programme is real, and the timeline is optimistic.

body.h2: The regulatory wall — and the crack in it

body.p: Since 1973, US federal regulation 14 CFR 91.817 has banned civil aircraft from exceeding Mach 1 over land. It was written in response to the sonic boom, and it is the single biggest reason Concorde could only ever be an ocean-crossing aircraft. Without overland routes, a supersonic fleet's network economics shrink dramatically.

In June 2025 an executive order directed the FAA to remove that ban and replace it with a noise-based standard. That is a genuine opening — but as of spring 2026 the FAA had still not published a final noise certification rule, and had not published a completed type certification plan for Overture. The door is unlocked; nobody has walked through it yet.

Boom's answer is Boomless Cruise: flying at around Mach 1.3 at an altitude where the shockwave refracts upward and never reaches the ground — a real physical effect known as Mach cutoff. XB-1 demonstrated it, going supersonic six times without a boom being heard below. It is clever, and it is also conditional on weather and atmospheric conditions, which makes it a scheduling variable rather than a guarantee.

body.h2: The plot twist: Boom is currently selling power stations

body.p: The strangest development of the last year has nothing to do with aeroplanes. Boom took the Symphony core — designed to run at high temperature for hours of supersonic cruise — and repackaged it as Superpower, a 42 MW industrial gas turbine for AI data centres, sharing roughly 80% of its hardware with the aviation engine.

The orders arrived immediately. Crusoe placed a $1.25 billion launch order for 29 turbines, over 1.21 GW of generating capacity. A $300 million funding round followed, led by Darsana Capital with Altimeter, ARK Invest, Bessemer, Robinhood Ventures and Y Combinator. Boom says this is the remaining capital it needs to finish Symphony and continue Overture's certification.

Read that carefully, because it cuts both ways. The optimistic reading: the AI power boom is funding the supersonic airliner, and every hour the turbine core runs on a test stand is an hour of data for the engine that will fly. The pessimistic reading: the fastest-growing part of a supersonic aircraft company is now a business that has nothing to do with flight, and management attention is finite.

body.h2: And the rest of the field

body.p: Boom is not alone. NASA's X-59, built by Lockheed Martin's Skunk Works to prove that a sonic boom can be softened into a 'thump', made its first flight on 28 October 2025 and had completed nine flights by April 2026, working up to around Mach 0.95 — it has not yet gone supersonic. Its results are meant to inform the FAA's future noise rules, so in a real sense every supersonic airliner is waiting on it.

Further out, Hermeus received an FAA special airworthiness certificate in March 2026 for Quarterhorse Mk 2.1, a step toward an eventual Mach 5 aircraft. That is a different order of difficulty entirely, and a much longer horizon.

Honestly? Probably — but later and slower than the press releases suggest, and that is not the insult it sounds like. A demonstrator has flown supersonic. A factory exists. An engine is in manufacturing. Three airlines have reserved 130 delivery positions. Every one of those is more than any supersonic project has assembled since the 1960s.

But the orders are non-binding commercial interest, not firm capital. The engine has not run. The FAA has not written the rule. Certification has not started in earnest. Any one of those could add years, and history says at least one of them will.

The realistic read from mid-2026: expect Overture in the sky as a prototype before the decade is out, and expect fare-paying passengers in the 2030s. It will be slower than Concorde, quieter than Concorde, and — if Boom is right about the economics — it will do the one thing Concorde never managed, which is make money.
Concorde was a technical triumph and a commercial catastrophe. The comeback will only count if the next one is the other way round.
