compareMarquee: The 747 invented the widebody and ruled for thirty-eight years; the A380 was built to dethrone it and arrived exactly as the market it was designed for evaporated. The A380 wins every dimension that can be measured — longer wings, taller, heavier, hundreds more seats — and lost the argument anyway. Point-to-point flying dissolved the hub-and-spoke logic both aircraft were built on, and the twin-engine 777 and 787 finished the pair of them off.

The most consequential rivalry in aviation, and the numbers are almost boringly close — a metre here, half a metre there. That is not coincidence: both are sized to the same gates, the same airbridges, the same 150-seat sweet spot. The real difference is under the skin. The A320 was fly-by-wire from 1987. The 737 is a 1967 airframe stretched, re-engined and recertified for six decades — and that constraint is precisely why the MAX's engines sit so far forward on the wing.

The two aircraft that finally made the four-engine airliner extinct. Both are majority-composite, both are twins, both fly further on less fuel than anything before them. The A350 is the bigger machine — longer, wider, heavier, more seats — and it exists specifically to answer the 787. The 787 got there first, and in this industry being first is worth a very great deal.

Same manufacturer, same job description, twenty years apart. The 777 is bigger in every dimension and carries far more people; the 787 flies nearly as far on dramatically less fuel and opened routes too thin for a 777 to ever fill. It is the clearest illustration on this site of what composites and modern engines actually bought aviation — not more speed, not more size, but more range per seat.

The only two aircraft ever built to swallow cargo nothing else could take. The An-225 was a one-off, built to carry the Buran space shuttle on its back, and it remains the heaviest aircraft ever flown — 640 tonnes against the Galaxy's 381. It was destroyed at Hostomel in February 2022. The C-5 is less romantic, vastly more numerous, and still flying.

The Tu-144 flew first, by two months, and that was rather the entire point of it. It was also faster on paper and worse in almost every way that mattered: it needed afterburners just to hold cruise, it was so loud inside that passengers passed notes, and it carried fare-paying passengers for a grand total of 55 flights. Concorde flew commercially for 27 years. Being first is not the same as being right.

The most instructive fight in the widebody market, because it pits a 1992 aluminium airframe against a 2009 composite one — and the old aircraft is still selling. The 787 flies significantly further and burns less fuel. The A330neo costs considerably less to buy, and for a great many routes that is the only number that wins.

Boeing's own succession, and a quiet act of self-destruction. The 747 is longer, taller, and has the upper deck; the 777 hauls a comparable load on two engines instead of four, which is the whole story. The 777-300ER never beat the 747 on a spec sheet — it beat it on the fuel bill, and that was enough to end the four-engine era at the company that invented it.

The two heaviest aircraft that ever flew, built for opposite reasons. The An-225 is longer and heavier and lifts more; the A380 has the greater wingspan, a genuine second deck, and has carried over 850 people at once. One was built a single time for a space programme. The other was built 251 times for a market that never arrived.

The 757 is the aircraft Boeing cannot replace, and this comparison shows why. It is substantially longer than any 737, but its real advantage never appears on a spec table: enormous wing area and oversized engines, which let it climb out of short, hot, high-altitude runways and still cross the Atlantic afterwards. The 737 MAX 10 and the A321XLR are both, in their different ways, attempts to rebuild it.

The 767 pioneered ETOPS and made the twin-engine ocean crossing legal; the 787 was built to replace it and does nearly everything better. The interesting part is that the 767 refuses to die — it is still in production as a freighter, because a 1981 aluminium fuselage is cheap, simple and perfectly adequate for carrying boxes that do not complain about the cabin.

The subsonic and the supersonic answers to the same question. The B-52 is a 1952 design that will fly into the 2050s — eight engines, no supersonic pretensions, a bomb truck that has outlived every single aircraft designed to replace it. The Tu-160 is the heaviest and fastest combat aircraft ever built, a Mach 2 swing-wing with nearly twice the weight. One is still in production. The other has simply refused to stop.

The strategic airlifter versus the tactical one. The C-5 is far larger and can carry the C-17's payload and then some — but it needs a proper runway to do it. The C-17 is the compromise that actually gets used: less capacity, but it can put 77 tonnes onto a short unpaved strip and then reverse itself back out. Capability on paper against capability where the war actually is.

The two aircraft fighting over the 100–150 seat gap that Boeing and Airbus abandoned — the market that cost Bombardier so much that it handed the A220 to Airbus for a single dollar. The A220 is bigger, flies further and has a genuinely wide cabin for its class. The E2 is lighter, cheaper to run and easier to fill. Both are dramatically better than the regional jets they replace.

The current heavyweight bout, and the one that decides widebody flying into the 2040s. The 777-9 is the largest twin ever built — longer than a 747 — with a folding wingtip so that it can still use an ordinary airport gate. The A350-1000 is smaller, lighter, certified and already in service. Boeing's aircraft is more capable and years late; Airbus's is flying and taking the orders.

Two trijets, a decade apart, killed by the same thing. The L-1011 was the more advanced aeroplane — it could land itself in 1972 — and Lockheed lost so much money building it that it left the airliner business forever. The MD-11 flew faster and further and still missed its own range promises, and by the time the fixes worked, ETOPS had made the third engine pointless.

The most brutal comparison on this site. In 1976, Concorde crossed the Atlantic at Mach 2.04. In 2026, the newest airliner Boeing can build crosses it at about Mach 0.85 — and the 777X is the better aircraft by almost every commercial measure. It carries four times the passengers, flies twice as far, and does not need a government to subsidise its existence. Aviation did not fail to get faster. It decided not to.

The two workhorse outsize freighters of the Cold War, and they are far closer than either side ever cared to admit. The An-124 is longer, spans wider, and lifts more — 150 tonnes against the Galaxy's 122. The C-5 has nose-and-tail loading, which means it can be filled from both ends and turned around faster.
